author: @axle.australian.patriot
text:
Similar to Here in Australia, but we had an upgrade of the laws not to long back and they became more restrictive than the laws previously in place, 200W and MAX 20km/h.
Most ebikes and escooters I see on the street are easily getting up to 40 and some 60km/h, so they are not inside of the legal limits.
>
In Australia these higher powered units come under a similar category to a Moped (traditionally between about 25cc - 50cc petrol engine equivalent) requiring essential road worthy items such as lights, braking ability and some minimum frame and wheel requirements. These can be used on the roads on a Car license and must be registered.
The new Australian laws don't appear to correctly include mopeds any more (an ebike with greater capacity than 200W) and now jump straight to a motor cycle class which no ebike or scooter is capable of meeting the strict roadworthy requirements.
Put simply they muffed it up. Most are quite happy to pay the small registration fee of a moped, and to be fair requiring at a minimum of a car licence makes sense if they are interacting with traffic the same as any other vehicle.
>
I did have some interest in creating a small solar powered "power assist" bike or solar powered moped, but new Au laws stopped me in my tracks :(
The difficult part of that is everyone zips past me in the street on there ebikes at 60km/h and the police turn a blind eye to it, but go to the next town and they may not be as lenient and dish out fines. If laws are discretionary then they are not really laws.

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Speed limit is already around those of e-bikes, but as you don't have pedal assist you're actually falling under moped laws.

Legality or not, though, the problem is going to arrive when you get into an accident.  If you're on the road, and it's considered "illegal", you're gonna be automatically in the wrong.  If you're on the pavement and you run into a pedestrian, you're going to be in the wrong in pretty much all cases, I think.  IANAL, of curse.

Basically, I wouldn't worry too much about the legality of it, but I would make sure that you have insurance cover and your insurers are OK with covering you on it.  Then don't carry your driving license and have enough cash to cover an eventual on the spot fine :)

author: @rikhughes6452
text:
I remember the first 50cc sports mopeds    before them we had LPA which stood for "light pedal assistance" and these were old Raleigh type pedal and pop  . in order for a 16 year old to ride a moped it had to gave pedals to ride like a bicycle   the Japanese mopeds were really well, designed and certainly did not need pedal assistance and our generation had them without the law of today regarding restricted horse power  but the law was outdated so we had to have pedals that worked  in order to pass the mot and the boys in blue could use the law when we got lippy with them     it was all just a joke   but we played the game  tuned our 50cc engines FS1Es and AP 50s  the main ones and lads swore they got the elusive 60 MPH out of them  but I dont think they quite did  
The point im getting is   electric bikes legal    scooters in general apart from odd city experiments  are illegal   so what we need is a scooter with some sort of pedal attachment that hardly works but makes it a electric bike     that way it would as it did in our time force the law   to work it all out  it could have ridiculous small cranks that move it at 1mph whilst peddaling  but   it might past the law   and the company that does it right   makes a killing

author: @FloatingIdeasonanarrowboat
text:
Interesting. I've been making my folding bike electric. I've had most of the bits for years though. In that time the UK e-bike law changed. In 2016 they removed the option of twist and go (throttle control). Apparently, throttles are only allowed to take you up to walking speed now. I think that was to enable less able riders to set off. So, as a scooter cant use pedal assist, they cant just lump a scooter into the same rules existing for e-bikes.
It will be interesting to see what the outcome is. PS I agree it looks far more appealing and simple than a granddad mobility thingy.

author: @xspotbox4400
text:
There are no 250W motors, watts don't have much to do with motor capabilities. It all depends on motor controller electronics. People were driving bicycles powered by a single, small drone motor, so...
Why did they install a bicycle seat on this scooter, it's uncomfortable when you don't need to pedal.
Why did they install disk brakes, you could brake just fine with motor regenerative breaking or with a simple rubber.
That frame tubing is the heaviest and largest part on a scooter, it could be converted to a battery cells storage.
And so on and on, there so many things that could be implemented in those emerging technologies, many were demonstrated on this channel. The sad fact is, there are no good scooters on the market, probably because people who manufacture them are not the same people who depend on cheap and bionic like transportation.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
Independent of any consideration of the power source.
Why a scooter and not a bicycle with a seat? The latter is safer, more stable, more practical, more comfortable to ride, and potentially better at carrying a load. The larger wheels of a bicycle are better able to deal with the ruts and potholes of British roads.
I condend, It is the fact that it is a scooter that makes it a toy. Maybe the standing riding position is supposed to imply it is safe to ride on a pavement along with pedestrians, however the growing weight and power output means it is increasingly dangerous to mix with pedestrians.  It really is questionable if the standing position would ever be safe on the road, with the side wind from passing traffic. The seat fitted to the model shown in the video, shows the devices are evolving to be more practical, actually returning to the concept of the Lambretta.
The scooters  may be safe weaving between very slow moving, or stationary traffic, but it will never be as safe as a bicycle.
 I suspect that if this mode of transport is to be favoured in necessary noise city centres, its speed will need to be reduced, and that may be its downfall, as it maybe seen as no more than a version of those invalid vehicles used by the elderly, and that will certainly not be seen as cool or trendy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I disagree mate - a little scooter is somehow more convenient for short hopping

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering is it any more convenient than a folding bicycle?
Your suggestion that it is "somehow more convenient" might be a clue, as I suspect that you are misinterpreting the fun factor as convenience. 
It's problem is that it must evolve, it has already evolved from the skateboard, to the motorised skateboard. I contend that it is this path of evolution that has allowed it to find a legal loophole, which is now being filled. There is a fundamental limitation with its utility, the need to be able to carry something, be it a briefcase or a shopping bag. 
Scooters have found an application, in moving people around large factor spaces, but in such locations the environment and road surface is very controlled. The same cannot be said of a town or city pedestrian area. 
It is perverse that the bicycle is limited to the road, where these less stable means of transport might be allowed to mingle with pedestrians of all ages. 
I cannot deny that the scooter maybe fun for the younger generation to use, but that will not make them safe. They were potentially dangerous when unpowered, they are even less safe when powered. There is a problem with stopping distance and momentum. I contend that someone sitting on a bicycle is in a better position to deal with there excess momentum than someone standing on a scooter. At speed, the latter is forced to dismount and run to dissipate their excess momentum, before coming to a stop. The abandoned scooter continues in a straight line, until its momentum is dissipated. If the scooter is fitted with a seat, it is more like the old Lambretta, and that was never legal to use on the pavement. 
It is the evolution of this mode of transport that is the problem, as its utility demands it change into something unacceptable to mix with pedestrians.

author: @JerryPaulTreeCreations
text:
I love that you're looking into these. I've owned an electric bike, (well, about 4), since the early 2000's, when they first became available. During the summer months, if I don't have to go on any long trips, I don't even license my truck. I've literally saved thousands of dollars!
This May, I bought what is called an "EMMO Monster". Its an 84 volt, scooter style, electric bike. So far, this one has become my favorite of all of them. It has 3 speed modes, alarm system, all necessary lights as well as emergency 4-way flashers, and on top of all that, it has a bluetooth/fm radio stereo system. 
I can't stop at a store or the post office without people asking me about it and where they can get one! In fact, one of the gentlemen I spoke with, stopped by my place to show me the one he bought shortly after talking with me. Now, I see him scooting around all over town with a giant grin on his face. ... This guy is nearly 70 years old!
I've sent so many people to the dealership where I bought it, they've decided to pay me a referral fee for everyone who uses my name when getting one. Now, (to use your words  😉), that's AWESOME! 😊
Cheers, from Saskatchewan, Canada.

replies.text: Exactly!! I find it disgusting how greedy governments are that with all the rising costs involved with travel, they can’t let this one go. Bicycles will get a tax at some point too. As revenue will inevitably decline from cars and those bike lanes weren’t free.

replies.author: @Alacritous
replies.text: @MrFas4wd It costs money to administer the government. If the government has to do something specific to this device that doesn't happen with other things, then they have to pay for it somehow.

replies.author: @saxum2171
replies.text: THIS IS WHY ENOUGH IS ENOUGH IM DONE WITH THE POLITICIANS & IM DONE WITH THE STUPID MONARCHY

replies.author: @frankhooper7871
replies.text: Well, e-bikes aren't taxed (other than the VAT you pay when buying one), and non-hybrid electric cars don't pay VED, so I doubt that's actually an issue.

author: @Dorsetwatersofteners
text: If it had peddles would it be legal? Dynamo peddles?

replies.author: @bruceh4833
replies.text:
Pedals to make it a bicycle with pedal assist and an interchangeable 250W to 12KW motor.  They won't know unless you're speeding! ;)  

Stop!  Or, I'll say Stop again!

text: Don't get it.....range 25 km, top speed 25 km/h, but 4 hours run time...hmmmm....i want one anyway
